# Questions in Livestream 216

Live Stream #216: Rock Around The Ark Tonight

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=628" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:28</a> | What do you make of Kenneth Kitchen explicitly denying northern locations in his Avaris and Raamses discussion? | He says Kitchen rejects Tell el-Daba as Pi-Ramesses but not as Avaris, and instead locates Ramesses farther north near Qantir. | He says he personally discussed the issue with Kitchen while serving as his research assistant, and Kitchen's rejection of northern options seemed more emotional than evidential. Kitchen was attached to Leonard Woolley and Ur of Sumer, while the speaker thinks the northern locations are much stronger than the southern option. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=783" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:03</a> | Why is the river in Joshua 24:2 usually taken as the Euphrates instead of the Jordan? | Because the patriarchal setting points east toward Mesopotamia, so he reads the river there as the Euphrates, not the Jordan. | He reads the verse in context: Terah, Abraham, and Nahor are connected with Mesopotamian origins, and verse 3 says Abraham was taken from beyond the river and led through Canaan. Since that northern Mesopotamian setting is not naturally described as beyond the Jordan, he takes the river as the Euphrates. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=1048" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:28</a> | If I could travel back to the ancient Near East, what would impress me more: vowels and punctuation, or sandwiches and grilled cheese? | He says the food would be more striking, since familiar punctuation is less impressive than discovering ancient flatbread-and-cheese combinations. | He jokes that ancient texts lacked vowels, punctuation, and even spaces, but ancient food would also disappoint modern sandwich expectations. They had bread and meat combinations, but not sliced bread or modern sandwiches; even grilled cheese would be closer to rubbery halloumi on flatbread than a familiar modern sandwich. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=1209" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:09</a> | Could the nodules on Amenhotep II have been a postmortem reaction to embalming materials? | He says no; he thinks the marks reflect a pox-like condition, possibly smallpox, that Amenhotep II died from rather than long-survived scars or embalming effects. | He argues the marks look like a plague or pox condition also seen on other royal mummies, not an embalming reaction. Because the lesions do not appear as old scar tissue, he thinks Amenhotep II probably contracted the condition near death rather than surviving it for years. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=1614" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">26:54</a> | Is the people determinative on the Merneptah Stele a problem for an early-exodus or judges-period dating of Israel? | No. He says it marks Israel as an ethnos rather than a settled place-name, which still fits an Israel not yet fully urbanized. | He explains that the people determinative and Asiatic throwing-stick sign mark Israel as a wandering people group rather than a city or land. He also points to the poetic pairing on the stele, where Israel is coupled with another non-urban people, suggesting a stage before Israel is being represented as a city-holding polity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=1803" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:03</a> | What are your thoughts on the Sabu disc? | He treats it as an odd but probably mundane artifact, not persuasive evidence of lost advanced technology. | He describes it as a carefully carved stone object that looks almost metallic, but he rejects ancient-alien or high-technology explanations. His preferred explanation is a large three-wick oil lamp, possibly imitating an earlier bronze form, because comparable folded single-wick lamps are known in Egyptian material culture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=1938" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:18</a> | Any thoughts on making a response video to Scott Stripling's Second Exodus Conference lecture? | He has not watched the lecture yet, so he is not ready to respond to it. | He says he has not finished watching the lecture, though he already has concerns that Stripling is drawing heavily from weak YouTube sources. He mentions Kramer, Titus Kennedy, and the Amenhotep II boils argument as examples of material he was not impressed by, but stops short of a full response because he has not completed the lecture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=2094" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:54</a> | Could the decreasing ages in Genesis reflect nearer ancestors being remembered individually while earlier ones were merged? | He says that is broadly possible within a clan-style reading, since genealogical compression can make nearer figures more individualized. | He allows the proposed memory-based explanation, but says he leans more toward urbanization changing the function of clans. As people move into cities, clan structures become less necessary for defense and social organization, so the apparent lifespan of clans and clan identities can diminish across the genealogical tradition. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=2391" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">39:51</a> | Why would clan members stop trying to keep a clan together and instead split into sections inside cities? | Because urban life breaks clans into mixed neighborhoods and new economic ties, so city growth naturally erodes older clan cohesion. | He says cities force people into jobs, trades, and neighborhoods that are not organized around preserving clan integrity. In rural farming communities, land and kinship can reinforce each other, but cities mix clans, tribes, and ethnic groups together; empires could also break clans deliberately through deportation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=2654" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">44:14</a> | In Proverbs 7:6-9, is the young man used to these streets and people or not? | He reads the scene as a naive young man drifting into danger rather than someone seasoned in that environment. | He treats the passage as wisdom literature presenting a representative young man who lacks sense and is vulnerable to ruin. The point is not that the man is streetwise, but that he is too naive to avoid the dangerous path and the woman who comes to meet him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=2923" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:43</a> | Which biblical retelling of the exodus gets overlooked most? | He points to Moses's birth in Exodus 2, reading the basket among the reeds as an overlooked foreshadowing of Israel's later passage through the reeds. | He says the overlooked retelling is inside Exodus itself, so readers miss it as a narrative within the narrative. Moses is placed in a reed basket among the reeds and drawn out of the water, which he reads as Moses personally passing through the sea of reeds before Israel later does so corporately. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=3164" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:44</a> | What is your favorite retelling of the exodus narrative? | In Scripture, he says the crucifixion is his favorite retelling because Christ is the Passover lamb; outside Scripture, he still likes The Ten Commandments despite its flaws. | He identifies the crucifixion as the strongest scriptural exodus retelling because Christ is the Passover lamb who takes away sin. For films, he still picks The Ten Commandments, though he says the long romantic subplot bloats the movie and would need cutting for the film to work better. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=3366" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">56:06</a> | How is baptism spiritually efficacious? | He says baptism is a promise: without true commitment it has no efficacy, but if the promise is kept, God keeps the believer in the faith. | He frames baptism as a promise to live for Christ rather than a magical act that works apart from faithfulness. He rejects automatic baptismal regeneration as he understands it, arguing that many people have been culturally baptized without spiritual renewal, so efficacy is tied to the real promise and life of faith. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=3690" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:01:30</a> | How is communion spiritually efficacious? | He says communion is spiritually efficacious when it is received in true worship and real fellowship with Christ and other believers, not as mere bread and wine. | He treats communion as a covenantal act of fellowship, worship, and participation rather than a bare snack or an automatic mechanism. Its benefit comes when the communicant approaches it rightly, in faith and communion with Christ and the church. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=3930" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:30</a> | Are baptism and communion simply symbolic? | No. He says both are signs with real covenant force, not mere reminders with no spiritual effect. | He distinguishes signs from mere empty symbols: baptism and communion signify covenant realities and carry real spiritual seriousness when received faithfully. At the same time, he rejects the idea that the outward act mechanically saves or benefits someone apart from genuine faith. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=4730" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:18:50</a> | Could you explain the towns in Joshua being described as forts? | He says the Hebrew term can cover anything from an unwalled hamlet to a fortified city, so each place has to be evaluated in context. | He cautions against assuming that every term translated as city or fort means the same kind of settlement. The Hebrew vocabulary can cover a range of habitation types, so archaeology and context have to decide whether a site was a small village, an unwalled town, or a fortified center. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=4853" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:53</a> | Is the Sermon on the Mount about the kingdom of heaven? | Yes, but he says it is also about Christ's kingdom on earth and, more centrally, about raising the standard so high that it shows people's need for God. | He says the sermon is certainly kingdom teaching, but not in a way that lowers the law into an easy moral program. It intensifies the standard, exposing the heart and showing that people need divine help rather than merely external righteousness. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=5679" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:34:39</a> | Did Pharaoh's daughter find Moses in Avaris? | Possibly in that broader delta setting, since he places the Hebrews in that region. | He does not present the location as certain, but says the larger delta setting is plausible because he locates the Hebrews in that area. If the royal women were using waterways in that region, Avaris or its broader environs could fit the kind of scene described. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=5764" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:36:04</a> | Why is Joseph shaved before seeing Pharaoh? | Because Egyptian court etiquette required a shaved appearance, especially in a culture that linked hair with dirt, lice, and lack of refinement. | He says shaving was part of Egyptian court cleanliness and presentation, especially before appearing before Pharaoh. Hair could be associated with dirt and lice, so Joseph's grooming marks his transition from prison condition to acceptable Egyptian court appearance. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=5828" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:37:08</a> | Does "shaved" there mean clean-shaven or just trimmed? | He takes it as a full shave, not a light trim. | He reads the wording and Egyptian custom as indicating a complete shave rather than simply neatening or trimming the beard. The point is full conformity to Egyptian court expectations, not just basic grooming. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=5917" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:38:37</a> | How did Egyptians shave? | They used bronze razors, including convex disc-like blades sharpened on leather. | He explains that Egyptians used bronze razors and maintained them by sharpening or stropping them, including on leather. The tools could include curved or disc-like forms, so clean shaving was technologically practical in that setting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=6045" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:45</a> | Did the Hyksos adopt Egyptian practices too? | Yes. He says they adopted a number of Egyptian customs while still remaining a foreign ruling group. | He says the Hyksos took on Egyptian practices because they ruled in Egypt and had to operate within Egyptian court and administrative culture. Adoption of customs did not erase their foreign identity, but it shows acculturation during their rule. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=6131" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:42:11</a> | I've never seen an Indiana Jones movie. Should I? | He says yes, as long as you treat them as fun adventure movies rather than realistic archaeology. | He recommends watching them as enjoyable adventure films, not as a model for archaeological method. Their value is entertainment, while the archaeology is exaggerated and often detached from real fieldwork. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=6576" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:36</a> | What is the most unrealistic thing about archaeology in the Indiana Jones movies? | He says the speed and drama are wildly unrealistic, especially the idea that major finds happen through solo derring-do instead of slow documentation. | He says real archaeology is slow, team-based, and dominated by careful recording, paperwork, and context. The films compress that into constant danger, immediate discoveries, and individual heroics, which makes for good cinema but bad archaeology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=7025" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:57:05</a> | Is Genesis 13:7 an anachronism? | He does not think the verse creates a serious problem and treats such wording as compatible with later scribal updating. | He does not see the wording as a serious chronological objection by itself. He treats such place-name or people-name references as the sort of updating a later scribe could make for readers without undermining the underlying patriarchal setting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=7098" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:58:18</a> | Are there parallels between Horus's birth and Moses's birth? | He says the parallels are weak and not persuasive as a meaningful source relationship. | He jokes that the only real parallel is ordinary birth, then distinguishes multiple Horus traditions rather than treating "Horus" as a single story. Once the actual Egyptian birth traditions are considered, he sees no substantive literary dependence between Horus's birth and Moses's birth. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=7547" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:05:47</a> | Why is Abimelech called king of the Philistines in Genesis 26:1? | He leans toward retrojection or later labeling rather than taking it as firm proof of full Iron Age Philistines already being there. | He says the phrase is best read as a geographic retrojection that tells the reader the story is set on the coastal plain. It does not mean Abimelech's people were ethnically Iron Age Philistines; the later name helps readers identify the region. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=7695" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:08:15</a> | Why do some biblical writers seem to lean on Leviticus while others lean on Deuteronomy? | He says that could reflect preference, local access, or inspiration, but the writers evidently knew both legal traditions. | He first says the question is unclear, then distinguishes Leviticus as focused on temple and priestly practice and Deuteronomy as a second-law presentation for life in the land. In the follow-up, he says access is hard to prove, but the biblical writers appear to know both bodies of law. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=7756" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:09:16</a> | What do you think of Christine Hayes's Old Testament lectures? | He has not watched enough of them to offer a real opinion. | He identifies Hayes as a Hebrew Bible scholar and knows of her lecture series, but says he has not watched enough to evaluate it. His answer is deliberately limited: he does not offer praise or criticism because he lacks firsthand exposure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=7944" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:12:24</a> | What are your thoughts on the Christus Victor model of atonement? | He says he does not like it because he sees it as tied to ransom-theory assumptions and, in some uses, vulnerable to low Christology. | He objects to Christus Victor when it depends on the idea that Satan owned humanity or the world and Christ had to win ownership back from him. He also worries that some versions slide toward low Christology, where Christ is treated as a mere man who becomes victorious and then deified. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=8120" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:15:20</a> | Is Christus Victor different from simply saying Christ is victorious over death and hell? | Yes. He says all orthodox atonement views affirm Christ's victory, while Christus Victor as a model is specifically tied to ransom-theory ideas about Satan holding humanity hostage. | He says every orthodox atonement model affirms that Christ triumphs over death and hell, so that alone does not define Christus Victor. The distinct model, as he describes it, is bound to ransom theory and the claim that Christ's work is directed at freeing humanity from Satan's supposed control. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=8306" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:18:26</a> | What denominations most clearly teach Christus Victor? | He associates it most strongly with Eastern Orthodoxy and some related traditions. | He says Christus Victor and ransom-theory language are more common in Eastern Orthodoxy than in western churches, which tend more toward penal atonement. He also notes that some Anglican strands have revived similar language, especially as ransom theory has reappeared in recent decades. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=8890" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:28:10</a> | Who needs a pastor if you can have an AI pastor? | He says some people are already using AI for therapy-like or spiritual roles, but he warns that AI tends to flatter and reinforce users rather than tell them hard truths. | He says people are already sharing intimate details with AI as if it were therapy or guidance, and the system often responds by validating and flattering them. That makes it a poor substitute for a pastor, because it tends toward sycophantic reinforcement rather than truthful correction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=9318" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:35:18</a> | How much does AI impair academic research going forward? | He says it is seriously damaging research because students who rely on AI for writing do not learn the material or build the thinking habits needed for later scholarship. | He argues the danger is not simply that AI gives answers, but that students use it to bypass the process of learning to research and write. If students outsource papers and only disguise the output, they enter later academic work without the knowledge, judgment, or habits scholarship requires. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=9840" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:44:00</a> | Is there a spell-check program you would recommend over Grammarly? | He says to use a basic spell-checker, like the one in Word, and avoid Grammarly-style tools that start rewriting your style for you. | He recommends ordinary spell-checking rather than tools that reshape sentences and prose style. His concern is that style-rewriting tools can make writers dependent on the tool and flatten their own voice or judgment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=9963" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:46:03</a> | Where can I find the AI paper you mentioned? | He says he had seen it mentioned in a recent video and then looked up and posted the video link in the chat. | He does not recite a full citation in the answer. He says he encountered the discussion through a recent video, looked up the relevant item, and put the video link in the chat for viewers to follow. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=10172" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:32</a> | Did ordinary Egyptians remove shoes before their superiors? | No. He says that was not a normal social custom in Egypt. | He rejects shoe removal as a routine Egyptian gesture of deference before social superiors. In his answer, removing sandals belongs to sacred-space logic rather than ordinary court etiquette. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=10292" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:51:32</a> | Was Isaac basically calm about the sacrifice? | Probably not. He treats Isaac as a real participant in a frightening event, not as someone casually fine with it. | He pushes back on readings that make Isaac emotionally detached or serenely compliant. The binding would have been terrifying, and Isaac should be read as a real person caught in a traumatic event rather than as a flat symbol. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=10885" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:01:25</a> | How should we understand the binding of Isaac? | He says the question is too large to answer adequately in a short livestream response and notes that the passage has generated huge debate. | He declines to give a full treatment because the Akedah is too complex for a brief livestream answer. He notes that the passage has a vast interpretive history and that any responsible answer would need more time than the format allows. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=11026" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:03:46</a> | Did the people of Avaris practice child sacrifice? | He says there is no evidence that they did. | He says the archaeological and historical evidence does not show child sacrifice at Avaris, either among the Hyksos or the people who followed them. The answer is framed negatively: there is no basis to claim that practice for the site. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=11058" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:04:18</a> | Could Genesis 25:2 support the Midianite hypothesis? | Only in a limited way. He says the verse uses toponymic regional names, so it is not strong evidence by itself, though someone might press it in that direction. | He defines the Midianite hypothesis as the idea that Yahweh originated as a Midianite deity brought to Israelites in Egypt. Genesis 25:2 names Midian among Keturah's children, but he says many of those names are regional toponyms retrojected onto ancestors, so the verse can be used only weakly and does not prove the hypothesis. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=11305" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:08:25</a> | Why did Jacob send others ahead in groups before meeting Esau? | To soften Esau up and protect himself. He says Jacob was ingratiating himself and effectively putting buffers between Esau and himself. | He reads the messengers and gifts as Jacob trying to win favor and avoid being killed by Esau. Later he sharpens the point: the first groups soften Esau's anger, while the later spacing also functions as a defensive buffer between Esau and Jacob. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=11705" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:15:05</a> | Why couldn't Isaac take the blessing back? | Because the blessing was a one-time covenantal appeal before God, so once given to the designated heir it was final. | He says the firstborn blessing is made before God as a covenantal appeal that asks God to approve and bless the designated heir. Since there cannot be two firstborns and the blessing has already been invoked, Isaac cannot simply revoke it after discovering the deception. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=11896" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:18:16</a> | Did Esau really want the blessing rather than the birthright? | Yes. He says the blessing effectively restored the birthright anyway, so Esau could afford to despise the birthright as long as he expected the blessing. | He distinguishes the birthright as the material inheritance from the blessing as the public and divine stamp of approval. Esau could gamble away the birthright because, if he later received the blessing, that blessing would effectively return the status and goods to him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=12096" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:21:36</a> | Is Jacob testing God in Genesis 28:20-22? | Maybe; he says Jacob is making a conditional vow or deal with God, which was common but not a spiritually mature practice. | He reads Jacob's vow at Bethel as deal-making with God after the ladder vision, not as the most mature expression of faith. He notes that Jacob sets up a standing stone and makes a conditional promise, a kind of vow that God may honor but that still reflects spiritual immaturity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=12451" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:27:31</a> | Is making this kind of deal with God a good practice? | No. He calls it spiritually immature and says people should not make deals or oaths to God that they may not keep. | He says deal-making with God is common in human history but badly advised. He gives Hezekiah as an example of a request that brought more years but also later disaster through Manasseh, using that to warn against trying to bargain with God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=12559" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:29:19</a> | Can you make a conditional oath to God? | Yes, but he warns that if the condition is met, the oath must be kept. | He says conditional oaths are possible, but the person making one is bound if God meets the condition. He uses Jephthah as a cautionary example and concludes that it is wiser not to make such oaths or treat God like a vending machine. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=12822" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:33:42</a> | Which Levitical laws applied to all Israel, and was Deuteronomy for all Israel or just Levites? | He says it depends on the specific law: some commands were priestly, while others were for the whole nation. | He says the answer has to be handled law by law and in context. Some Levitical instructions apply specifically to priests and temple service, while others address the whole nation; Deuteronomy is not simply a manual for Levites alone. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=12867" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:34:27</a> | When should a believer be baptized? | He prefers baptism soon after a genuine conversion rather than delaying it for a long time. | He says New Testament examples often show baptism following belief quickly, and he prefers that pattern. Baptism is the first act of faith after conversion and brings the believer publicly into the community of Christ, so it should not be postponed without good reason. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=12980" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:36:20</a> | How are Hebrew and Egyptian poetry different? | He says they are actually quite similar, since both rely heavily on couplet-like structures. | He says the two traditions are more alike than different in this respect. Both use parallel, couplet-style structures, so readers familiar with biblical poetic parallelism have a useful starting point for understanding Egyptian poetry as well. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=13670" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:47:50</a> | If Jephthah's daughter was sacrificed, why did she lament her virginity? | He says she was lamenting her lost posterity and legacy, not entering a vestal-virgin role, because Israel did not have such a category. | He says the lament is about dying without descendants and therefore losing posterity, not about being assigned to a perpetual virgin office. He rejects a vestal-virgin style category for Israel and reads the grief through lineage and legacy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=14010" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:53:30</a> | Is animal sacrifice a form of blood magic? | No. He treats it as covenantal sacrificial worship, not magic. | He distinguishes biblical sacrifice from magic by grounding it in covenant worship and divine command rather than manipulation of spiritual forces. Blood is significant within the sacrificial system, but not as a technique for coercing power. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=14075" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:54:35</a> | Could you explain Spirited Away and Totoro? | He reads Spirited Away as dealing with child exploitation and Totoro as a layered story about death in an agricultural community. | He gives interpretive readings rather than plot summaries: Spirited Away concerns a child drawn into exploitative labor and adult corruption, while Totoro has a gentler surface that he reads as shadowed by illness, death, and rural communal anxieties. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=14369" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:59:29</a> | Is that Totoro interpretation confirmed by the creators? | He says Miyazaki did not state it overtly, but he sees it as suggested by comments about the news story that inspired the film. | He says the interpretation is not officially stated in a direct, explicit way by Miyazaki. Still, he thinks creator comments about the real news story behind the film make the darker reading plausible as a subtext rather than a confirmed authorial statement. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=14812" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:06:52</a> | Does the Rumpelstiltskin story have ancient or demonic roots? | He is doubtful and says he does not know of a solid ancient-demonic line behind it, beyond later folklore development. | He does not know evidence for an ancient demonic origin behind the tale. He treats it as later folklore development and remains skeptical of attempts to derive it from a specific ancient supernatural tradition. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=14919" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:08:39</a> | Do the "to this day" verses in Joshua help date the book? | A little. He says they can set a rough horizon for some passages, but not enough to settle the dating question by themselves. | He says "to this day" notices can show that a statement was written or updated after the event and while the condition still held. They may give a rough lower horizon for a passage, but they do not by themselves solve Joshua's overall date. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=15022" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:10:22</a> | Why did Israel keep taking on the gods of defeated peoples? | He says that reflects the general pull of polytheism: once many gods are assumed to be available, people keep adding more rather than staying exclusive. | He explains that in a polytheistic mindset, defeating or encountering another people does not necessarily disprove their gods. People can simply add more divine patrons or powers to the mix, so Israel's temptation was syncretism rather than exclusive loyalty to Yahweh. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=15258" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:14:18</a> | Since Torah means law or instruction, how should we understand "law" in the New Testament? | He says Torah really does mean law, and the New Testament understands it through Christ, who fulfills the ceremonial law and is Lord of the Sabbath. | He resists softening Torah into something other than law, while also saying Christians read the law through Christ's fulfillment. Ceremonial law is fulfilled in him, and Sabbath questions must be understood under Christ's lordship rather than as a simple repetition of old covenant practice. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=15421" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:17:01</a> | Did the Old Kingdom end because of a mega-drought, and how could that happen if Egypt lived off the Nile? | He says drought played a role, but the real collapse came from political decentralization, nomarch power, and civil war. | He says environmental stress matters, but Egypt did not collapse from drought alone. The more decisive issue was a political breakdown in which regional nomarchs gained power, central authority weakened, and conflict fractured the kingdom. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=15595" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:19:55</a> | Why do we only see the sandal-removal command here and in Exodus 3? | He says it marks sacred space, but especially because leather sandals were animal products and thus ritually unclean for temple-like holy ground. | He connects the command to sacred ground and temple-like holiness rather than ordinary etiquette. Leather sandals come from animal products, and he treats their removal as a purity action appropriate when standing before God's holy presence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=15772" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:22:52</a> | Did Moses think he was meant to be a deliverer when he killed the Egyptian, or did he just lash out in anger? | He says it was an act of anger and overreaction, not a justified deliverer move. | He rejects the idea that Moses's killing of the Egyptian should be celebrated as the proper beginning of deliverance. He reads it as anger and overreaction, showing Moses acting wrongly before God's appointed deliverance rather than executing a justified mission. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=15900" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:25:00</a> | How does Genesis point to the exodus, and how do the rest of the Old and New Testaments point back to it? | He says Genesis lays ritual and covenant foundations that culminate in Exodus, and later Scripture keeps reusing the exodus as the central redemption pattern. | He says Genesis prepares the exodus through covenant promises, ancestral movements, and ritual foundations that become clearer in Exodus. Later biblical writers repeatedly return to the exodus as the paradigm for redemption, judgment, deliverance, and God's identity as savior. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=15941" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:25:41</a> | If plastic is made from dead dinosaurs, is it ritually unclean like leather? | He says petroleum-derived materials are treated as ritually clean because the material has been processed and purified by the earth. | He answers playfully but seriously within purity logic: plastic is not treated like leather because it is not an intact animal product used in that form. The long natural processing and refinement of petroleum means it is not ritually equivalent to wearing leather sandals. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=16107" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:28:27</a> | Were any parts of the pre-exilic books written from Babylon, and how would we know? | He says you would look for signals such as Persian or other exile-era loanwords, which can point to a Babylonian setting or later composition horizon. | He says evidence would come from linguistic and contextual signals, especially loanwords and features that fit exile or post-exile settings. Persian or other later terms can suggest a Babylonian or later editorial horizon, though they need to be evaluated case by case. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=16201" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:30:01</a> | Is Rahab's story a Passover reference? | Yes, he sees a real Passover echo in the rescue logic and the marked household. | He reads Rahab's marked house and household rescue as echoing Passover. The sign on the house marks those inside for deliverance while judgment falls outside, making it more than a generic escape story. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=16460" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:34:20</a> | Why frogs in the plagues? What is so bad about frogs? | He says frogs were a water-supply problem: dead or toxic frogs in wells, houses, and stored drink could poison what people depended on. | He emphasizes the practical disaster rather than comic annoyance. Frogs entering homes, vessels, and water sources would foul drinking supplies, and dead or toxic frogs would make the plague a serious public-health and survival problem. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=16650" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:37:30</a> | Does the name Yahweh itself imply an unmoved mover? | He does not think the divine name by itself proves that philosophical idea. | He does not derive the full philosophical concept of an unmoved mover directly from the divine name. The name may speak to God's being and self-existence, but he does not treat it as sufficient proof of that later metaphysical formulation by itself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=16715" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:38:35</a> | Is Genesis 1 a polemic against polytheism? | Yes, in part. He likes reading it as a demythologizing text that strips rival divine powers of real godhood. | He says Genesis 1 can be read as demythologizing the cosmos: sun, moon, waters, and other things worshiped elsewhere are presented as created things under God's command. That makes it polemical against polytheism without needing to turn every line into a direct attack on a named deity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=16901" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:41:41</a> | How do you think Sarah handled the binding of Isaac afterward? | He assumes she would have been deeply upset rather than casually at peace with it. | He assumes Sarah would have responded as a mother to a horrifying event involving her son. The transcript does not support a detailed reconstruction, but he rejects any bland reading in which Sarah simply accepts it without deep distress. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=17063" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:44:23</a> | Is the Muslim God the same as the Christian God? | No. He says the attributes, gospel, and Christology are different, so shared name-language is not enough to make them the same God or Christ. | He argues that shared monotheistic language is not enough when the identity and attributes of God are defined differently. In particular, Islamic rejection of the Trinity, the gospel, and Christian Christology means he does not identify Allah as the same God confessed in Christianity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=18144" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">5:02:24</a> | Have you heard people refer to the Holy Spirit as "the Lord"? | Yes. He says it is not incorrect, since the Holy Spirit is called God in the New Testament and "Lord" translates Yahweh. | He says calling the Holy Spirit "Lord" is theologically acceptable because the Spirit is divine. Since Lord is used for Yahweh and the New Testament identifies the Spirit with God, the title is not improper even if some listeners are less used to hearing it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=18244" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">5:04:04</a> | Who commissioned Vatican II? | He thinks it was probably Pope John XXIII, but says he could be wrong and treats the answer as uncertain. | He answers cautiously from memory and says he thinks John XXIII commissioned or initiated Vatican II. He explicitly leaves room for correction rather than presenting the answer as certain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5bDfwDUvOiQ?t=18311" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">5:05:11</a> | How do you keep your goatee so even? | He says he combs it and trims it with scissors, though sometimes it ends up shorter than other times. | He gives a practical grooming answer: he combs the goatee out and trims it with scissors to keep the sides even. He adds that it is not always perfect and sometimes ends up shorter than intended. |
